title: Have the Academy Awards Changed for Good? A Post-‘Parasite’ Oscars Mailbag
format: Sean and Amanda work through a listener mailbag on the wreckage of the 92nd Academy Awards: Neon vs. A24, whether Parasite's win could ever repeat, and the boring-front-runner slate of 2020. Then Sean talks to Celine Sciamma about Portrait of a Lady on Fire.

films.title: Parasite
films.note: The frame for the whole mailbag. Sean and Amanda argue it won in spite of the subtitle and foreign-language hurdles, not because of them, and doubt a sweep like it happens again, citing Adam Nayman's Ringer piece on how unusually accessible it is.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: The Netflix film they keep circling back to: seen at home in conditions no one associates with Oscar movies, which they suspect hurt it. Tom Quinn's line about seeing it in a theater comes up as the counter-case for distributors like Neon.

films.title: Roma
films.note: The Green Book / Roma question: they doubt Parasite wins if Roma had won the year before, because the subtitled-foreign-film lane would already have been spent. Amanda thinks Netflix over-pitched it with shot-by-shot mailers.

films.title: Green Book
films.note: Sean's example of the actual underdog of its year: the film that, for all intents and purposes, shouldn't have won.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: One of the three films they name from the 2019 festival run alongside Parasite and Portrait; Brad Pitt's supporting win recurs in the acting-category talk.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: Grounds the Greta Gerwig makeup-Oscar question: it made money and made her a brand, which they think pulls her toward writing-directing over acting.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: Cited on both Netflix's aggressive campaign and Laura Dern's supporting win, which Amanda and Sean don't read as a vote for the film itself.

films.title: Joker
films.note: The Leo counterfactual: even without a Revenant win on the books, they think Joaquin Phoenix's reception was uniform enough that he still takes Best Actor.

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: Scott's question pairs it with Parasite as the surprising, validating kind of Best Picture win.

films.title: The Shape of Water
films.note: The other side of Scott's question, grouped with Green Book as the boring, uninspired winner.

films.title: Midnight Cowboy
films.note: Sean's pick for the most radical Best Picture ever: an X-rated film that kicked off New Hollywood, a world away from the prior year's Oliver!

films.title: Shakespeare in Love
films.note: Their landmark in the negative sense: the win over Saving Private Ryan that made the public aware of the campaign as a political event, via Harvey Weinstein.

films.title: Crash
films.note: Named as a landmark before they table the full Crash conversation for a later question.

films.title: West Side Story
films.note: First on the 2020 boring-front-runner short list: Spielberg adapting a monument of Hollywood history, which Amanda flags as the year's 1917 slot.
The reference point for the whole front-runner list: the big, technically ambitious, end-of-year Oscar movie.

films.title: The Trial of the Chicago 7
films.note: Sorkin's real-life political drama with a big cast chewing his dialogue; they expect it on the fall festival circuit.

films.title: News of the World
films.note: Tom Hanks and Paul Greengrass reuniting after Captain Phillips, a Christmas period piece. Sean draws the same 1917 comparison but says it sounds more like a good movie than an uninspired front-runner.

films.title: The French Dispatch
films.note: Wes Anderson's New Yorker riff; Sean's cautious about the premise but glad for any excuse to revisit Anderson.

films.title: Dune
films.note: Villeneuve's, which Sean concedes is unfair to lump in: big sci-fi rarely does well at the Oscars, with Avatar about the only counterexample.

films.title: The Father
films.note: Florian Zeller's stagey dementia drama from Sundance; Sean says if Anthony Hopkins isn't nominated he'll be shocked, and calls it the closest thing on the list to the question's uninspired-showcase description.

films.title: Pulp Fiction
films.note: Sean's answer to which Tarantino most deserved Best Director: 1994, against Forrest Gump, even if it's his pinnacle alongside Inglourious Basterds.

films.title: Inglourious Basterds
films.note: Amanda's favorite Tarantino, but she can't argue against Kathryn Bigelow or James Cameron in the 2009 field.

films.title: Kill Bill: Vol. 1
films.note: Amanda rewatched it sick in bed and rates it 100 out of 100; anchors the look at the 2003 Best Director slate.

films.title: The Revenant
films.note: Leo's win, the pivot for Alex's counterfactual about an Oscar-less DiCaprio in 2019. Sean calls it his best, most lived-in performance.

films.title: Killers of the Flower Moon
films.note: The Scorsese/DiCaprio David Grann adaptation, then heading into production; Sean reads the period-injustice-thriller shape as very Oscar-y.

films.title: Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri
films.note: Frances McDormand as the case study for role-vs-performance and personality: people, Sean argues, just liked what she was about.

films.title: Judy
films.note: Renée Zellweger, revisited on whether voters were rewarding a performance or just wanting to give Judy Garland something.

films.title: Honey Boy
films.note: Shia LaBeouf's, which Sean thinks was a snub: the first step on his long road to Oscar acceptance, now streaming on Amazon.

films.title: The Peanut Butter Falcon
films.note: The other 2019 LaBeouf film; the warmth for it and his telecast appearance read as him willing to play the game.

films.title: Ford v Ferrari
films.note: Sean's stand-in for a Ringer-staff preferential-ballot winner, and the example of the sound-category split (it took editing, 1917 took mixing).

films.title: Tenet
films.note: The Nolan question: Sean bets against it but vows to reset his feelings on Nolan this summer after talking Dunkirk with Tarantino.

films.title: Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse
films.note: The template for the Sean-and-Amanda one-off pod they're being asked to repeat; the bar for a movie ‘as ill as Spider-Verse.’

films.title: Miss Americana
films.note: The Netflix Taylor Swift documentary Sean says he hated fully and would delete from the service, mostly to bait Amanda.

segments.heading: Celine Sciamma on ‘Portrait of a Lady on Fire’

films.title: Portrait of a Lady on Fire
films.note: Sciamma's first feature not centered on adolescence: an 18th-century lesbian love story she built around shot-reverse-shot, since the painter faces the model. Set during the Enlightenment partly to surface a suppressed generation of women painters. She wrote it scene-by-scene, starting from the final image, and lit the castle interiors entirely from outside: a rig of some 20 spotlights faking natural window light, plus rope lights for the candle scenes. Painter Hélène Delmaire, found on Instagram, made the canvases; each took ~80 hours.

films.title: Girlhood
films.note: The film after which she declared her first three a coming-of-age trilogy; structured like five 26-minute TV episodes, a rhythm she credits to loving TV series.

films.title: Water Lilies
films.note: Her 35mm debut, written at 26, about the very beginning of adolescence.

films.title: Tomboy
films.note: The end-of-childhood entry, shot cheaply on an early Canon DSLR: part of building each film as a low-cost prototype.

films.title: Persona
films.note: The one film she'll admit quoting: a Bergman frame she calls a wink. Two women trapped in an island home; she feels Portrait shares its DNA.

films.title: Rebecca
films.note: One of the Hitchcockian textures Sean hears in the patient, face-withholding reveal of Adèle Haenel's character.

films.title: Suspicion
films.note: Named in the same breath as Rebecca for that suspenseful early-encounter mood.

films.title: Barry Lyndon
films.note: Sean's touchstone for the window-framed, natural-looking light, which Sciamma reveals was 20 spotlights doing the job.

films.title: Carol
films.note: Sciamma compares the cult intensity around Portrait to what Todd Haynes told her about people's relationship to this film, both lesbian love stories that had been missing from screens.

films.title: Mulholland Drive
films.note: The film she built her own cult around. She reads it as simple, a dream of the second half, and takes from it that ‘I love you’ is a sentence that always has a future, an idea she chased writing Portrait.

films.title: Gone with the Wind
films.note: One of her examples of the greatest films in history being love stories, however uninteresting cinematically.

films.title: Titanic
films.note: Same list: proof of the universal appetite for love stories she wanted to confront as a genre.

films.title: Pretty Woman
films.note: Rounds out her love-story trio, from a country she says barely even makes romantic comedies anymore.
